transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Bryce. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess I've been thinking a lot about my personal projects, like this website I've been wanting to build. But, honestly, I'm not sure if it's worth it. I mean, what's the point if no one will see it or appreciate it? It just feels... pointless sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you've been feeling uncertain about your website project and questioning its value, especially if you’re worried about whether others will notice or appreciate it. That sense of it feeling "pointless" can be really discouraging. Can you share a bit more about what inspired you to want to build this website in the first place?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I thought it could be a way to express myself and share some of my ideas. I have a lot of interests, and I thought it might be nice to have a space where I could put them all together. But then I start thinking about whether anyone would actually care to look at it. I guess I just feel like if it’s not for some kind of recognition or validation, then maybe it’s not worth doing at all. It’s kind of frustrating.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the website represents a meaningful outlet for your self-expression and interests, but you're grappling with the idea of recognition and validation from others. That frustration is understandable—wanting your efforts to be appreciated can be a powerful motivator. When you think about expressing yourself through the website, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think there’s a mix of emotions. Sometimes I feel excited about the possibilities and what I could create. But then, that excitement quickly turns into sadness and disappointment when I think about how it might just go unnoticed. It’s like I want to share something important, but I also feel this weight of doubt, like maybe it’s not really worth it if it’s just for me. It’s a bit of a rollercoaster, honestly.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That mix of excitement and sadness sounds really intense. It’s like you’re on this emotional rollercoaster, where the thrill of creation is often overshadowed by doubts about its value. It’s understandable to want your work to resonate with others, but it seems like there’s also a part of you that values the importance of sharing your ideas, even if it’s just for yourself. Can you tell me more about what those ideas or interests are that you’d like to share?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I have a lot of different interests, actually. I enjoy writing about technology and how it impacts our lives, and I also like exploring creative projects, like photography. There are so many things I want to say and share, but I guess I worry that they won’t be interesting enough to anyone else. Sometimes I think about how I could combine those interests on the website, but then I just end up feeling stuck. It’s like I have all these ideas swirling around, but I can’t seem to get them out in a way that feels meaningful.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a rich tapestry of interests, from technology to photography, and you’re eager to express those ideas. Yet, the fear that they might not resonate with others leaves you feeling stuck. That can be a frustrating place to be, especially when you have so much you want to share. When you think about the process of combining those interests into something meaningful, what do you think holds you back the most?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think a big part of it is that fear of judgment. I worry that if I put my ideas out there, people might not get them or might think they’re not good enough. It’s like I have this inner critic that keeps telling me I shouldn’t even bother if it’s not going to be perfect. And then there’s the procrastination—I just end up scrolling through social media instead of working on the website. It’s easier to avoid it than to face that fear, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that fear of judgment and the pressure for perfection are really weighing on you, leading to procrastination as a way to avoid those uncomfortable feelings. It’s understandable to want your work to be well-received, but it seems like that fear might be holding you back from even starting. When you notice that inner critic speaking up, how does it make you feel about your ideas and your ability to share them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: When that inner critic chimes in, it definitely makes me feel smaller, like my ideas don’t matter. It’s discouraging, and I start to doubt myself and my abilities. I think, “Why would anyone want to read what I have to say?” It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of feeling inadequate, and that just makes it harder to even try. I want to believe in my ideas, but that voice just keeps pushing me down. It’s frustrating because I know I have something to say, but it feels like I’m my own biggest obstacle.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to feel that way, especially when you know you have valuable ideas to share. That inner critic seems to create a cycle of self-doubt that makes it hard for you to take the steps you want to take. It’s frustrating to feel like you’re your own biggest obstacle when you have so much to offer. When you think about that voice and how it affects you, are there moments when you feel more confident or able to push through those doubts?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: There are moments, yes. Sometimes when I’m just lost in the creative process, like when I’m writing or taking photos, I can feel that spark of confidence. It’s like I’m in my own little world, and I forget about the judgment for a while. But those moments can be fleeting. Once I step back and think about sharing it, that critic comes back in full force. I wish I could hold onto that feeling of confidence longer, but it’s hard to shake off the doubts. It’s like I have to fight to keep that spark alive.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you experience those moments of confidence and flow when you’re engaged in your creative process. It sounds like those times are really special for you, but it’s frustrating that they can feel so fleeting. Holding onto that spark amidst the doubts can be a real challenge. When you’re in those creative moments, what do you think helps you feel that confidence and connection to your ideas?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s when I’m fully immersed in what I’m doing—when I’m not thinking about the end result or how others will perceive it. It’s like I’m just creating for the sake of creating, and that feels freeing. Sometimes, I’ll look back at what I’ve done and feel a sense of pride, even if I’m not ready to share it with anyone. It’s almost like I can tap into that excitement and passion when I let go of the pressure to be perfect or to seek validation. But then, as soon as I step back and start thinking about sharing it, that pressure comes back, and it’s hard to stay in that creative headspace.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that immersion in the creative process allows you to connect with your passion and excitement, free from the weight of expectations. That sense of pride in your work is a beautiful feeling, even if you’re not ready to share it yet. It’s understandable that stepping back to think about sharing brings back that pressure. What do you think it would take for you to hold onto that feeling of freedom and excitement when considering sharing your work with others?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would help to shift my focus from how others might perceive my work to just valuing the act of creating itself. Maybe if I could remind myself that it’s okay if not everyone connects with it, I could feel more comfortable sharing. I guess finding a way to separate my self-worth from the reactions of others could be key. It’s just hard to break that habit of seeking validation. I wonder if setting smaller, more personal goals could help, like sharing with just a few close friends first, instead of thinking about a wider audience. That might make it feel less daunting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach. Shifting your focus to valuing the act of creating for its own sake, rather than tying it to external validation, could be a powerful way to reclaim that sense of freedom. Setting smaller, more personal goals, like sharing with a few close friends, seems like a gentle way to ease into sharing without feeling overwhelmed. It’s understandable that breaking the habit of seeking validation takes time and practice. How do you feel about starting with those smaller goals? What might be the first step you’d like to take?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think starting with smaller goals feels more manageable. Maybe I could share a piece of writing or a photo with a couple of friends who I trust and who I know will be supportive. That way, it wouldn’t feel like such a big leap. It’s a little scary, but I think it could help me see that my work does have value, even if it’s just to a few people. I guess the first step would be to choose something I’m proud of and just send it to them. It feels a bit daunting, but I think it could be a good way to test the waters.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great plan, Bryce. Sharing something you’re proud of with trusted friends can be a meaningful way to take that first step. It’s natural to feel a bit scared, but it seems like you’re recognizing the potential value of your work, even if it’s just for a few people. Testing the waters in this way could help you build confidence and see that your ideas do resonate with others. How do you feel about identifying that piece you’d like to share? What might help you in that process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think identifying that piece could be a bit challenging, but I’m willing to give it a try. Maybe I could look through some of my recent writing or photos and see what stands out to me. It might help to remind myself that it doesn’t have to be perfect; it just needs to be something I feel good about. I could set aside some time to reflect on what I want to share and why it matters to me. Having that intention might make it feel more meaningful and less about what others think. I guess taking that time to connect with my work could be a good first step.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful approach, Bryce. Setting aside time to reflect on your work and what feels meaningful to you can help you connect with your creativity and intentions. It’s great that you’re reminding yourself that it doesn’t have to be perfect; just focusing on what you feel good about is a powerful mindset. Taking that time to explore your pieces could also help you build confidence in what you want to share. How do you feel about scheduling that time for yourself? What might that look like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think scheduling that time could be really beneficial. I could set aside an hour or so this weekend to go through my writing and photos. Maybe I could find a quiet space where I won’t be distracted, just to really focus on what resonates with me. I could bring a notebook to jot down my thoughts and feelings about each piece. It might help to create a little ritual around it, like having a cup of tea or some music in the background to make it feel special. I think having that dedicated time could help me connect with my work and make the process feel more intentional.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a lovely plan, Bryce. Creating a quiet space for yourself and adding those little rituals, like having tea or music, can make the experience feel more special and intentional. Setting aside that dedicated time to reflect on your work could really help you connect with what resonates with you. It’s great to hear you’re thinking about how to make it meaningful. How do you feel about the idea of this dedicated time? What are you most looking forward to as you prepare for it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m actually feeling pretty good about it. The idea of having that dedicated time feels like a step in the right direction. I’m looking forward to just being able to immerse myself in my work without the pressure of judgment. I think it’ll be nice to reconnect with my creativity and remember why I enjoyed these projects in the first place. I’m also curious to see what stands out to me and what I feel proud of. It feels like a chance to explore without any expectations, and that’s exciting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling good about this step! The opportunity to immerse yourself in your work without the pressure of judgment sounds like a refreshing way to reconnect with your creativity. It’s exciting that you’re looking forward to exploring what stands out to you and what you feel proud of. Embracing that sense of curiosity and allowing yourself to enjoy the process can be really liberating. As you prepare for this time, is there anything else you’d like to reflect on or consider that might support you in this journey?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to remind myself that this is just for me, and it doesn’t have to lead anywhere specific. I could also think about what I want to take away from this experience, whether it’s just a sense of accomplishment or maybe even some clarity about what I want to share. It might be good to write down a few affirmations or positive reminders to keep that inner critic at bay while I’m reflecting. I guess focusing on the joy of creating rather than the outcome could really help me stay grounded in the moment.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are excellent insights, Bryce. Reminding yourself that this time is just for you and doesn’t have to lead anywhere specific can help create a sense of freedom in the process. Writing down affirmations or positive reminders is a great idea to counter that inner critic and keep you grounded in the joy of creating. Focusing on the experience itself rather than the outcome can really help you stay present and connected to your work. It sounds like you’re setting yourself up for a meaningful and enriching experience. How do you feel about the affirmations you might want to write? What kind of messages would resonate with you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think some affirmations that resonate with me could be things like, “My ideas are valuable, even if they’re not perfect,” or “Creating is a journey, and it’s okay to explore without pressure.” I might also want to remind myself that it’s okay to share just for the joy of it, without needing validation. Something like, “I create for myself first, and that’s enough.” I think having those positive messages could really help me stay focused on the joy of the process and keep that inner critic in check. It feels empowering to think about writing those down.
